EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, but quickly moving area of precipitation in the form of freezing rain, sleet and snow, quickly moved from northern Iowa, into far southern Minnesota by 9 am Sunday morning, January 27th. Initially, heavy sleet occurred along the Iowa border, but as it moved northward into cooler air, the precipitation was in the form of snow and sleet.  By noon, most of the precipitation ended in far southern Minnesota, where up to a half inch of ice and sleet occurred near Albert Lea, with amounts between a tenth and a quarter of an inch from Albert Lea, northeast to Blooming Prairie and Bixby in southeast Steele County. ||As the precipitation moved into the Twin Cities metro area, and areas north of the cities, most of the precipitation turned quickly into snow, with a short period of heavy snowfall during the afternoon. ||Most of the accumulating snowfall ended by sunset, but lingering light snow and freezing drizzle occurred in portions of east central Minnesota through midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy sleet fell between 830 am and 10 am, but tapered off to freezing drizzle and freezing rain by late morning before ending around noon. Up to a half inch of sleet and freezing rain occurred around Albert Lea, and areas to the southeast, near the Iowa border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure with an Arctic air mass settled over the Midwest, a fairly unusual situation for early December. On the morning of the 7th, low temperatures across eastern North Dakota and portions of the northwestern quarter of Minnesota ranged from about 15 below to 25 below zero. When combined with steady west winds, wind chill readings dipped to 40 below to 55 below zero.

A couple rounds of snow (except along from Duluth and along the North Shore where lake enhancement maintained continuous snowfall) from December 2nd through early December 5th brought widespread snowfall totals of 10 to 18 to northeast Minnesota.  Higher amounts of over 20, and even over 30, fell near the Twin Ports and along the North Shore.  Snowfall reports included 33.2 near Two Harbors, 28.6 in Duluth Heights, 21.7 in Askov, 21.6 at the NWS office in Duluth/Hermantown, 18.5 in Cloquet, 17 in Moose Lake, 16.5 near Hinckley, 15.6 near McGregor, 14 in Grand Rapids, 11 in Fort Ripley and near Brainerd, 11 in Larsmont, 10.5 near Orr, and 8.8 near Grand Marais. Gusty east to northeast winds developed at the head of Lake Superior, which not only caused lake enhanced snow, but also periods of near blizzard conditions.  Duluth had a peak wind of 32 mph on the 4th.  There were power outages in parts of Duluth and Hermantown due to downed power lines and blown transformers.  Duluth area schools, colleges, and many other public venues were closed Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday.  The 21.6 of snow that fell at the Duluth NWS office was the 6th biggest snowstorm on record.  There was one death Monday (12/2) evening in Aitkin County when an elderly woman driving on icy Highway 210 slid out of her lane and was hit by an oncoming vehicle.

A couple rounds of snow (except along from Duluth and along the North Shore where lake enhancement maintained continuous snowfall) from December 2nd through early December 5th brought widespread snowfall totals of 10 to 18 to northeast Minnesota.  Higher amounts of over 20, and even over 30, fell near the Twin Ports and along the North Shore.  Snowfall reports included 33.2 near Two Harbors, 28.6 in Duluth Heights, 21.7 in Askov, 21.6 at the NWS office in Duluth/Hermantown, 18.5 in Cloquet, 17 in Moose Lake, 16.5 near Hinckley, 15.6 near McGregor, 14 in Grand Rapids, 11 in Fort Ripley and near Brainerd, 11 in Larsmont, 10.5 near Orr, and 8.8 near Grand Marais. Gusty east to northeast winds developed at the head of Lake Superior, which not only caused lake enhanced snow, but also periods of near blizzard conditions.  Duluth had a peak wind of 32 mph on the 4th.  There were power outages in parts of Duluth and Hermantown due to downed power lines and blown transformers.  Duluth area schools, colleges, and many other public venues were closed Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday.  The 21.6 of snow that fell at the Duluth NWS office was the 6th biggest snowstorm on record.  There was one death Monday (12/2) evening in Aitkin County when an elderly woman driving on icy Highway 210 slid out of her lane and was hit by an oncoming vehicle.
Band of lake effect snow brought about 6 inches of fresh snow to the Lutsen area along the North Shore in the Minnesota Arrowhead.

In the wake of a blizzard that moved across portions of west central Minnesota early Sunday morning, December 29th, strong winds developed and temperatures fell into the teens below zero. This caused wind chill values to drop significantly. This continued into Monday morning before temperatures rose to the single digits below zero, and wind speeds decreased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell into the teens below zero Sunday morning, December 29th. As winds continued to be gusty and average around 15 to 30 mph, wind chill values held near 35 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved rapidly southward across western Minnesota during the afternoon of Saturday, December 28th. Ahead of this front, temperatures warmed into the 30s and 40s allowing for some snow melt and a hard crust to form prior to the frontal passage. Temperatures dropped quickly into the teens and 20s during the afternoon with winds increasing to 30 to 40 mph. A few gusts were near or slightly above 50 mph at times during the height of this event. Surface visibility initially was good and averaged 2 to 4 miles.  However, once temperatures dropped into the single digits and winds increased to above 30 mph sustained, surface visibility dropped significantly. Several county officials, and local weather stations reported surface visibility very low, especially in open country along the Minnesota River Valley. Once the winds decreased Saturday evening, surface visibility improved. A county official reported white out conditions west of Danvers in western Swift County, but only windy conditions in the eastern part of the county. County officials also reported white out conditions in Chippewa, Lac Qui Parle, and Yellow Medicine counties. Although wind speeds increased to 25 to 40 mph across the rest of southwest Minnesota, due to the snow melt earlier in the afternoon, lack of additional snowfall and blowing snow, surface visibility was not bad, and averaged around 2 to 4 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials and weather station observations indicated blizzard conditions for several hours during the late afternoon, and evening hours of Saturday, December 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure with an Arctic air mass settled over the Midwest, a fairly unusual situation for early December. On the morning of the 7th, low temperatures across eastern North Dakota and portions of the northwestern quarter of Minnesota ranged from about 15 below to 25 below zero. When combined with steady west winds, wind chill readings dipped to 40 below to 55 below zero.

An arctic air mass held across the Upper Midwest during this period. As surface winds remained in the 5 to 10 mph range, and temperatures fell to near 20 below zero, wind chill values dropped to 35 to 40 degrees below zero. The coldest wind chill was -38 at both Staples and Long Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local observations in Stevens County had wind chill values averaging -35 degrees during the early morning hours of Saturday, December 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Upper Midwest had a long duration winter storm event that developed Monday morning, December 2nd, and eventually evolved into a large storm system that moved from the Plains, northeast across the Upper Midwest and western Great Lakes region by late Wednesday night. ||A weak disturbance produced locally two to six inches across central Minnesota from Long Prairie, eastward to Milaca, North Branch, and into Wisconsin from Monday morning to Tuesday morning. An isolated 8.2 inch total occurred north of Mora in northern Kanabec County.  Some of the precipitation changed over to rain in east central Minnesota as warmer air began to surge northward. ||By early Tuesday, much warmer air from the southern United States produced precipitation in the form of sleet, rain and snow that fell through late Tuesday evening across portions of south central and east central Minnesota. The mixture of precipitation eventually changed over to all snow Tuesday night, into Wednesday and produced a swath of 6 to 9 inches of snow from southwest Minnesota, northeast to central, east central Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin. ||The heaviest snow fell late Wednesday morning, into early Wednesday evening as the surface low tracked from eastern Kansas, north-northeast to Lake Superior. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although some precipitation fell Monday and Tuesday in the form of snow, sleet and rain, the bulk of the heavier snow fell early Wednesday morning, through Wednesday afternoon. This storm produced locally 6 to 8 inches of snow across the county.

Wind speeds started to increase late Sunday night December 22nd, and continued to gust during through the morning hours of Monday, December 23rd. Although wind speeds decreased to less than 10 mph Monday afternoon, the wind shifted to the south ahead of another weather system and began to gust during the early morning hours of Tuesday, December 24th. There were a few hours during the afternoon and evening of Monday, December 23rd when wind chill values were only averaging between 15 to 25 below and temperatures held in the -5 to -15 below range.  The coldest wind chill values occurred during the early morning hours of Monday, December 23rd where a few locations in the headwaters of the Minnesota River Valley dropped to near -45 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As temperatures fell below -10, and wind speeds averaged around 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values fell to around 35 to 40 below zero. Some of the gusts produced wind chill values as low as -45 Monday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations up to a quarter of an inch from freezing rain were reported across southeast Minnesota from a winter storm that impacted the region on January 27th. The winter storm originated over South Dakota and then moved almost due east into Wisconsin. As it did, it pulled a warm front north through the region. The warm air that came north with the warm front was responsible for the freezing rain. In addition to the freezing rain, sleet and some snow also fell. The freezing rain and sleet produced a coating of ice on most exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads with several accidents occurring. The highest reported ice accumulation was a quarter of an inch in Harmony (Fillmore County). The Minnesota State Patrol reported hundreds of accidents and spinouts across the southern part of the state. Ice buildup from the storm downed some power lines in Austin (Mower County) and brought down tree limbs in other parts of Mower County. Power outages also occurred in Rochester (Olmsted County) and more than 8 flights were canceled or delayed at the Rochester International airport. Nearly all schools across southeast Minnesota were either closed or delayed after the storm on Monday the 28th. Some roads remained ice covered into Tuesday the 29th when at least 3 school buses slid off the road but no injuries resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and sleet created a coating of ice on exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads and numerous accidents occurred across the county. The highest reported ice accumulation was a quarter of an inch in Harmony.

Ice buildup from the storm downed some power lines in Austin (Mower County) and brought down tree limbs in other parts of Mower County. Power outages also occurred in Rochester (Olmsted County) and more than 8 flights were canceled or delayed at the Rochester International airport. Nearly all schools across southeast Minnesota were either closed or delayed after the storm on Monday the 28th. Some roads remained ice covered into Tuesday the 29th when at least 3 school buses slid off the road but no injuries resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and sleet created a coating of ice on exposed surfaces leading to very slick roads and numerous accidents occurred across the county. The highest reported ice accumulation was 0.15 of an inch at the Rochester airport. At least a dozen people were treated at the Mayo Clinic for weather related injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure with an Arctic air mass settled over the Midwest, a fairly unusual situation for early December. On the morning of the 7th, low temperatures across eastern North Dakota and portions of the northwestern quarter of Minnesota ranged from about 15 below to 25 below zero. When combined with steady west winds, wind chill readings dipped to 40 below to 55 below zero.

Wind speeds started to increase late Sunday night December 22nd, and continued to gust during through the morning hours of Monday, December 23rd. Although wind speeds decreased to less than 10 mph Monday afternoon, the wind shifted to the south ahead of another weather system and began to gust during the early morning hours of Tuesday, December 24th. There were a few hours during the afternoon and evening of Monday, December 23rd when wind chill values were only averaging between 15 to 25 below and temperatures held in the -5 to -15 below range.  The coldest wind chill values occurred during the early morning hours of Monday, December 23rd where a few locations in the headwaters of the Minnesota River Valley dropped to near -45 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As temperatures fell below -10, and wind speeds averaged around 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values fell to around 35 below zero. Some of the gusts produced wind chill values as low as -40 Monday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Upper Midwest had a long duration winter storm event that developed Monday morning, December 2nd, and eventually evolved into a large storm system that moved from the Plains, northeast across the Upper Midwest and western Great Lakes region by late Wednesday night. ||A weak disturbance produced locally two to six inches across central Minnesota from Long Prairie, eastward to Milaca, North Branch, and into Wisconsin from Monday morning to Tuesday morning. An isolated 8.2 inch total occurred north of Mora in northern Kanabec County.  Some of the precipitation changed over to rain in east central Minnesota as warmer air began to surge northward. ||By early Tuesday, much warmer air from the southern United States produced precipitation in the form of sleet, rain and snow that fell through late Tuesday evening across portions of south central and east central Minnesota. The mixture of precipitation eventually changed over to all snow Tuesday night, into Wednesday and produced a swath of 6 to 9 inches of snow from southwest Minnesota, northeast to central, east central Minnesota, and into portions of west central Wisconsin. ||The heaviest snow fell late Wednesday morning, into early Wednesday evening as the surface low tracked from eastern Kansas, north-northeast to Lake Superior. Some of the higher snowfall totals include; south of Hillman in Morrison County with 9.2 inches, 8.5 inches near St. Michael in Wright County, and 7.8 inches south of Bird Island in Renville County of southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snowfall event, produced locally 6 to 8 inches across most of Mills Lacs County, with the heaviest in the northern portion. Some areas in the northern part of the county received a quick 6 inches Monday evening, but occasional light snow continued into Wednesday afternoon, December 4th, and produced the local amounts up to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of snow (except along from Duluth and along the North Shore where lake enhancement maintained continuous snowfall) from December 2nd through early December 5th brought widespread snowfall totals of 10 to 18 to northeast Minnesota.  Higher amounts of over 20, and even over 30, fell near the Twin Ports and along the North Shore.  Snowfall reports included 33.2 near Two Harbors, 28.6 in Duluth Heights, 21.7 in Askov, 21.6 at the NWS office in Duluth/Hermantown, 18.5 in Cloquet, 17 in Moose Lake, 16.5 near Hinckley, 15.6 near McGregor, 14 in Grand Rapids, 11 in Fort Ripley and near Brainerd, 11 in Larsmont, 10.5 near Orr, and 8.8 near Grand Marais. Gusty east to northeast winds developed at the head of Lake Superior, which not only caused lake enhanced snow, but also periods of near blizzard conditions.  Duluth had a peak wind of 32 mph on the 4th.  There were power outages in parts of Duluth and Hermantown due to downed power lines and blown transformers.  Duluth area schools, colleges, and many other public venues were closed Monday, Tuesday, and Wednesday.  The 21.6 of snow that fell at the Duluth NWS office was the 6th biggest snowstorm on record.  There was one death Monday (12/2) evening in Aitkin County when an elderly woman driving on icy Highway 210 slid out of her lane and was hit by an oncoming vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening of October 4th ahead of warm front that was lifting north out of Iowa. These storms produced locally heavy rains that caused flash flooding to occur across portions of Mower, Fillmore, Winona and Houston Counties. Mud slides closed roads between Pickwick and the city of Winona (Winona County) and washed out a portion of a county road northwest of Lanesboro (Fillmore County). Almost 6 inches of rain fell in about a six hour period in Preston (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded an underpass along Oakland Avenue in Austin.

The fire was reported as numerous thunderstorms with frequent lightning were moving across southeast Minnesota during the evening of October 4th. Two fire departments were on the scene for five hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front surging northward across the region brought several thunderstorms to west central Minnesota.  One of these rotating storms brought a damaging tornado to far eastern Traverse county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low topped thunderstorm tracked from south to north across central and east central Traverse county producing a brief tornado touchdown. The tornado touched down in a soybean field and tracked from south to northwest for about a half mile with a maximum path width of 70 yards.  The tornado struck a grove of tall pine trees with most of them uprooted and then moved near a residence causing damage to the siding and roof damage. Three other large pine trees were twisted at their bases and fell into the home damaging the side of the house and breaking a window. The tornado hit a farm building lifting it off of its concrete foundation and twisting it to the northwest with all four walls destroyed.  An electric pole was also downed and fell onto two pickup trucks breaking the window on one of them. The tornado then tracked to the northwest where it downed several more large trees.  The tornado also took a large piece of tin from the outbuilding and stuck it eight inches deep into the ground.  The tornado then tracked into a corn field and dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought that developed across southeast Minnesota in early September, ended during the first week of October. Between 1.5 and 5 inches of rain fell across the region, which lifted the drought status out of the severe category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought across the eastern portions of Houston County was downgraded to moderate after 3 to 5 inches of rain fell during the first week of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving winter storm system produced locally 6 to 11 inches in a small band across southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, November 5th. Tuesday morning, an area of low pressure moved from Kansas northeast across Iowa, reaching Wisconsin by early Wednesday morning. A band of snow developed along the northwest side of storm, with an enhanced area of forcing led to localized 6 to 11 inches of snow near Milroy, Redwood Falls, and Bird Island. Most areas northeast of this area only received 2 to 5 inches of snowfall, with lesser amounts southeast across south central Minnesota where more rain fell during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local observers reported over 6 inches of snowfall in a 6 to 12 hour period from a storm system moved across the central Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms over northeast Minnesota brought large hail and damaging winds on August 29th.  One thunderstorm produced an EF-1 tornado.  The tornado damage path began around 6:28 pm 1.7 miles north-northwest of Remer, MN and ended around 6:55 pm 5.0 miles west-southwest of Hill City, MN.  The tornado path was 12.8 miles long and occurred over about 27 minutes.  The damage path was through a rural, forested area of northeastern Cass County and northwestern Aitkin County, but it narrowly missed the town of Remer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the continuation of EF-1 tornado that passed from Cass County into Aitkin County, crossing the county border about a mile southwest of Cranberry Lake in the northwestern corner of Aitkin County.  The damage path was through a rural, forested area doing damage mostly to trees.  The damage decreased to EF-0 south of Holy Water Lake.  The damage path continued southeast to about 5 miles west-southwest of Hill City, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms developed Thursday late afternoon and evening, June 20th, mostly in west central Minnesota, and moved eastward. These storms produced some penny to quarter size hail, but the main event was damaging straight line winds and torrential rainfall during the late evening and overnight period. These storms moved across central and east central Minnesota after midnight, with some redevelopment in far south central Minnesota toward daybreak. ||Flash flooding was observed in portions of west central and central Minnesota where locally three to five inches of rain fell in a short period of time. The towns of Morris, Glenwood, Starbuck, Sauk Centre and Long Prairie were the hardest hit with numerous flooded roadways. ||Wind damage came in two different waves, the first was associated with embedded bow-echoes that moved across central Minnesota, while the second wave came from outflow from previous thunderstorms.||Strong outflow winds from previous thunderstorms affected four counties in central Minnesota, producing a wide area of damage. Winds exceeded 40 mph for nearly two hours in portions of Todd and Stearns Counties.  Peak gusts include 63 mph at Sauk Centre (Stearns County) at 0200, 60 mph in Clearwater (Wright County) at 0240, 55 mph at Rice (Benton County) at 0200, and 54 mph at Long Prairie (Todd County) at 0235.   A business in Long Prairie lost a roof during the height of the high winds. Debris was blown to the south, landing on cars in a parking lot, and numerous trees were blown down around Long Prairie.  Trees and power lines were downed across portions of Todd, Benton, Stearns and Wright Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Albert Lea had many streets barricaded due to water over the road.  The streets in town was basically ponding due to saturated ground.  Many stalled vehicles were reported.  In the county there were several roads, including County Road 61 and County Road 27, near Freeborn, which had water over the road.  The intersection of County Roads 14 and 74, north of Emmons, needed significant repairs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was reported at the corner of Highway 7 and County Road 1.

A business in Long Prairie lost a roof during the height of the high winds. Debris was blown to the south, landing on cars in a parking lot, and numerous trees were blown down around Long Prairie.  Trees and power lines were downed across portions of Todd, Benton, Stearns and Wright Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused Highway 169 near Elmore to close due to flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous Aspen tree branches were blown down.  A twelve inch diameter Poplar tree was snapped 20 feet above the ground level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper level disturbance tracked into portions of northwest and west central Minnesota, creating areas of heavy snow. Wadena received 9.0 inches of snow, Bemidji 8.3 inches, New York Mills 7.5 inches, and Sebeka 7.0 inches. The heavy snow resulted in near zero visibilities, vehicle accidents, and postponements of school activities.

After a round of about one to nine inches of light snow fell across portions of northwest and west central Minnesota on December 2nd and 3rd, a second round of snow fell on the 3rd through the 4th. This second batch of snow set up along an inverted trough, bringing about 5 to 14 inches of snow to the area. Slightly higher amounts, about 8 to 14 inches, fell along and north of the U. S. Highway 2 corridor. In addition to the snow, some light freezing rain occurred over portions of west central Minnesota. Northerly winds also became quite gusty, producing some blowing and drifting snow with visibilities below a quarter mile at times. Many schools were closed and other activities were cancelled.

A small complex of thunderstorms that developed across far southwest Minnesota during the morning of Thursday, September, 19th, moved northeast across southern |Minnesota. These storms produced straight-line winds across portions of southern  Minnesota from near Granite Falls to Hutchinson, while another severe storm developed across the western Twin Cities Metro area, and moved northeast. ||The severe storm that developed in the western Twin Cities Metro area, produced sporadic wind damage and large hail from near Buffalo, north and east to Monticello, Big Lake, to Zimmerman and Cambridge, to Rush City. It produced 2-inch diameter hail near Cambridge, Minnesota, while other reports ranged from penny, up to golf ball size hail from near Maple Lake to Big Lake and Zimmerman. Some brief damaging winds from the outflow of the severe storms produced wind damage in northeast Minneapolis, Andover, and Blaine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal stoplight was blown down at the intersection of Hanson Blvd, and Bunker Lake Blvd, in Andover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in far northeast South Dakota, and moved east-southeast across west-central, and into central and east-central Minnesota during the afternoon and evening hours of Tuesday, August 6th.||One of the storms was long-lived and produced a swath of very large hail from Morris, southeast to the Twin Cities Metro area aninto Wisconsin. Numerous vehicles and homes were damaged from golf ball, up to baseball size hail in the southern metropolitan area.  In Bloomington, hail smashed windows in the Wells Fargo Plaza, leaving broken glass in the 24-story building's entry ways. The storms also brought high winds, which knocked over large trees in areas across the Metro area. Xcel Energy reported over 70,000 customers from St. Cloud through the Twin Cities area were without power during the height of the storm.

A large ridge of high pressure built across the central part of the United States during the last week of August. Heat and humidity increased across the Upper Midwest starting the weekend of August 25th, and lasted until the latter part of the week with a string of 90+ afternoon temperatures, combined with dew points in the 70s, caused heat indices to rise above 100 degrees from Sunday, through Tuesday, August 27th. In the Twin Cities metro area, heat indices remained above 80 degrees overnight, and afternoon heat indices continued above 100 degrees through Thursday afternoon, August 29th.  There were not any reports of deaths due to heat as a primary or contributing factor.||The Minnesota State Fair was going on during the time period.  216 people required  treatment at medical stations at the fair for heat related illnesses, 10 of whom were transported to local area hospitals. In addition, several record high temperatures were observed, and a dew point temperature of 77 degrees on August 27th at 3:00 PM tied the MSP high dewpoint temperature record set on August 27, 1990. It also tied the record for highest dewpoint ever during the State Fair (77 degrees - August 28, 1955 and August 27, 1990). Minneapolis schools canceled all outdoor after-school athletics practices during this period.  The August 26th high of 96 degrees in the Twin Cities broke the 94-degree record set in 1948.||In Hennepin County, from the 25th through the 29th, there were 28 people who were treated for heat related illnesses, either as walk-ins at emergency rooms, or transported by ambulance to hospitals.   There were no known reports of heat illnesses treated outside Hennepin or Ramsey Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across the area rose above 90 degrees during the afternoon of Sunday, August 25th.  As dew point rose into the 70s, the heat indices surpassed 100 degrees for several hours during the afternoon, topping out around 105 degrees. During the evening, heat indices held above 80 degrees.  The heat and humidity continued through Tuesday afternoon before temperatures fell into the 70s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening of October 4th ahead of warm front that was lifting north out of Iowa. These storms produced locally heavy rains that caused flash flooding to occur across portions of Mower, Fillmore, Winona and Houston Counties. Mud slides closed roads between Pickwick and the city of Winona (Winona County) and washed out a portion of a county road northwest of Lanesboro (Fillmore County). Almost 6 inches of rain fell in about a six hour period in Preston (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 15 near Pickwick was closed due to numerous mud slides. At least two trees were pushed over County Road 17 by mud slides.

During the afternoon of Saturday, August, 31st, a complex of thunderstorms developed across southeast North Dakota, and moved into west central Minnesota during the late afternoon hours. These storms moved southeast across mainly western and southern Minnesota and produced large hail and damaging winds. Most of the severe storms were concentrated along the Minnesota River Valley during the early evening hours as a bow echo moved southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down on Highway 212 in Sacred Heart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Saturday, August, 31st, a complex of thunderstorms developed across southeast North Dakota, and moved into west central Minnesota during the late afternoon hours. These storms moved southeast across mainly western and southern Minnesota and produced large hail and damaging winds. Most of the severe storms were concentrated along the Minnesota River Valley during the early evening hours as a bow echo moved southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large branches, up to six inches in diameter, were blown down around Clontarf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Saturday, August, 31st, a complex of thunderstorms developed across southeast North Dakota, and moved into west central Minnesota during the late afternoon hours. These storms moved southeast across mainly western and southern Minnesota and produced large hail and damaging winds. Most of the severe storms were concentrated along the Minnesota River Valley during the early evening hours as a bow echo moved southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust occurred on the Minnesota Department of Transportation wind sensor near Sauk Centre.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure built across the central part of the United States during the last week of August. Heat and humidity increased across the Upper Midwest starting the weekend of August 25th, and lasted until the latter part of the week with a string of 90+ afternoon temperatures, combined with dew points in the 70s, caused heat indices to rise above 100 degrees from Sunday, through Tuesday, August 27th. In the Twin Cities metro area, heat indices remained above 80 degrees overnight, and afternoon heat indices continued above 100 degrees through Thursday afternoon, August 29th.  There were not any reports of deaths due to heat as a primary or contributing factor.||The Minnesota State Fair was going on during the time period.  216 people required  treatment at medical stations at the fair for heat related illnesses, 10 of whom were transported to local area hospitals. In addition, several record high temperatures were observed, and a dew point temperature of 77 degrees on August 27th at 3:00 PM tied the MSP high dewpoint temperature record set on August 27, 1990. It also tied the record for highest dewpoint ever during the State Fair (77 degrees - August 28, 1955 and August 27, 1990). Minneapolis schools canceled all outdoor after-school athletics practices during this period.  The August 26th high of 96 degrees in the Twin Cities broke the 94-degree record set in 1948.||In Hennepin County, from the 25th through the 29th, there were 28 people who were treated for heat related illnesses, either as walk-ins at emergency rooms, or transported by ambulance to hospitals.   There were no known reports of heat illnesses treated outside Hennepin or Ramsey Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across the area rose above 90 degrees during the afternoon of Sunday, August 25th.  As dew point rose into the 70s, the heat indices surpassed 100 degrees for several hours during the afternoon, topping out around 105 degrees. During the evening, heat indices held above 80 degrees.  The heat and humidity continued through Thursday afternoon before temperatures fell into the 70s.

Severe thunderstorms over northeast Minnesota brought large hail and damaging winds on August 29th.  One thunderstorm produced an EF-1 tornado.  The tornado damage path began around 6:28 pm 1.7 miles north-northwest of Remer, MN and ended around 6:55 pm 5.0 miles west-southwest of Hill City, MN.  The tornado path was 12.8 miles long and occurred over about 27 minutes.  The damage path was through a rural, forested area of northeastern Cass County and northwestern Aitkin County, but it narrowly missed the town of Remer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across Highway 65.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure built across the central part of the United States during the last week of August. Heat and humidity increased across the Upper Midwest starting the weekend of August 25th, and lasted until the latter part of the week with a string of 90+ afternoon temperatures, combined with dew points in the 70s, caused heat indices to rise above 100 degrees from Sunday, through Tuesday, August 27th. In the Twin Cities metro area, heat indices remained above 80 degrees overnight, and afternoon heat indices continued above 100 degrees through Thursday afternoon, August 29th.  There were not any reports of deaths due to heat as a primary or contributing factor.||The Minnesota State Fair was going on during the time period.  216 people required  treatment at medical stations at the fair for heat related illnesses, 10 of whom were transported to local area hospitals. In addition, several record high temperatures were observed, and a dew point temperature of 77 degrees on August 27th at 3:00 PM tied the MSP high dewpoint temperature record set on August 27, 1990. It also tied the record for highest dewpoint ever during the State Fair (77 degrees - August 28, 1955 and August 27, 1990). Minneapolis schools canceled all outdoor after-school athletics practices during this period.  The August 26th high of 96 degrees in the Twin Cities broke the 94-degree record set in 1948.||In Hennepin County, from the 25th through the 29th, there were 28 people who were treated for heat related illnesses, either as walk-ins at emergency rooms, or transported by ambulance to hospitals.   There were no known reports of heat illnesses treated outside Hennepin or Ramsey Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across the area rose above 90 degrees during the afternoon of Sunday, August 25th.  As dew point rose into the 70s, the heat indices surpassed 100 degrees for several hours during the afternoon, topping out around 105 degrees. During the evening, heat indices held above 80 degrees.  The heat and humidity continued through Tuesday afternoon before temperatures fell into the 70s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms over northeast Minnesota brought large hail and damaging winds on August 29th.  One thunderstorm produced an EF-1 tornado.  The tornado damage path began around 6:28 pm 1.7 miles north-northwest of Remer, MN and ended around 6:55 pm 5.0 miles west-southwest of Hill City, MN.  The tornado path was 12.8 miles long and occurred over about 27 minutes.  The damage path was through a rural, forested area of northeastern Cass County and northwestern Aitkin County, but it narrowly missed the town of Remer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees uprooted along Highway 210.

There were not any reports of deaths due to heat as a primary or contributing factor.||The Minnesota State Fair was going on during the time period.  216 people required  treatment at medical stations at the fair for heat related illnesses, 10 of whom were transported to local area hospitals. In addition, several record high temperatures were observed, and a dew point temperature of 77 degrees on August 27th at 3:00 PM tied the MSP high dewpoint temperature record set on August 27, 1990. It also tied the record for highest dewpoint ever during the State Fair (77 degrees - August 28, 1955 and August 27, 1990). Minneapolis schools canceled all outdoor after-school athletics practices during this period.  The August 26th high of 96 degrees in the Twin Cities broke the 94-degree record set in 1948.||In Hennepin County, from the 25th through the 29th, there were 28 people who were treated for heat related illnesses, either as walk-ins at emergency rooms, or transported by ambulance to hospitals.   There were no known reports of heat illnesses treated outside Hennepin or Ramsey Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across the area rose above 90 degrees during the afternoon of Sunday, August 25th.  As dew point rose into the 70s, the heat indices surpassed 100 degrees for several hours during the afternoon, topping out around 105 degrees. During the evening, heat indices held above 80 degrees.  The heat and humidity continued through Tuesday afternoon before temperatures fell into the 70s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in far northeast South Dakota, and moved east-southeast across west-central, and into central and east-central Minnesota during the afternoon and evening hours of Tuesday, August 6th.||One of the storms was long-lived and produced a swath of very large hail from Morris, southeast to the Twin Cities Metro area aninto Wisconsin. Numerous vehicles and homes were damaged from golf ball, up to baseball size hail in the southern metropolitan area.  In Bloomington, hail smashed windows in the Wells Fargo Plaza, leaving broken glass in the 24-story building's entry ways. The storms also brought high winds, which knocked over large trees in areas across the Metro area. Xcel Energy reported over 70,000 customers from St. Cloud through the Twin Cities area were without power during the height of the storm.

A small complex of thunderstorms that developed across far southwest Minnesota during the morning of Thursday, September, 19th, moved northeast across southern |Minnesota. These storms produced straight-line winds across portions of southern  Minnesota from near Granite Falls to Hutchinson, while another severe storm developed across the western Twin Cities Metro area, and moved northeast. ||The severe storm that developed in the western Twin Cities Metro area, produced sporadic wind damage and large hail from near Buffalo, north and east to Monticello, Big Lake, to Zimmerman and Cambridge, to Rush City. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down across southwestern Isanti County.

A small complex of thunderstorms that developed across far southwest Minnesota during the morning of Thursday, September, 19th, moved northeast across southern |Minnesota. These storms produced straight-line winds across portions of southern  Minnesota from near Granite Falls to Hutchinson, while another severe storm developed across the western Twin Cities Metro area, and moved northeast. ||The severe storm that developed in the western Twin Cities Metro area, produced sporadic wind damage and large hail from near Buffalo, north and east to Monticello, Big Lake, to Zimmerman and Cambridge, to Rush City. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure built across the central part of the United States during the last week of August. Heat and humidity increased across the Upper Midwest starting the weekend of August 25th, and lasted until the latter part of the week with a string of 90+ afternoon temperatures, combined with dew points in the 70s, caused heat indices to rise above 100 degrees from Sunday, through Tuesday, August 27th. In the Twin Cities metro area, heat indices remained above 80 degrees overnight, and afternoon heat indices continued above 100 degrees through Thursday afternoon, August 29th.  There were not any reports of deaths due to heat as a primary or contributing factor.||The Minnesota State Fair was going on during the time period.  216 people required  treatment at medical stations at the fair for heat related illnesses, 10 of whom were transported to local area hospitals. In addition, several record high temperatures were observed, and a dew point temperature of 77 degrees on August 27th at 3:00 PM tied the MSP high dewpoint temperature record set on August 27, 1990. It also tied the record for highest dewpoint ever during the State Fair (77 degrees - August 28, 1955 and August 27, 1990). Minneapolis schools canceled all outdoor after-school athletics practices during this period.  The August 26th high of 96 degrees in the Twin Cities broke the 94-degree record set in 1948.||In Hennepin County, from the 25th through the 29th, there were 28 people who were treated for heat related illnesses, either as walk-ins at emergency rooms, or transported by ambulance to hospitals.   There were no known reports of heat illnesses treated outside Hennepin or Ramsey Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across the area rose above 90 degrees during the afternoon of Sunday, August 25th.  As dew point rose into the 70s, the heat indices surpassed 100 degrees for several hours during the afternoon, topping out around 105 degrees. During the evening, heat indices held above 80 degrees.  The heat and humidity continued through Thursday afternoon before temperatures fell into the 70s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary held across far southern Minnesota during the early morning hours of Thursday, August 22nd.  Thunderstorms developed along this front and slowly sagged southward across Brown County.  Some of the thunderstorms produced large hail stones and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down near Sleepy Eye.

Most of the severe storms were concentrated along the Minnesota River Valley during the early evening hours as a bow echo moved southeast.

There were not any reports of deaths due to heat as a primary or contributing factor.||The Minnesota State Fair was going on during the time period.  216 people required  treatment at medical stations at the fair for heat related illnesses, 10 of whom were transported to local area hospitals. In addition, several record high temperatures were observed, and a dew point temperature of 77 degrees on August 27th at 3:00 PM tied the MSP high dewpoint temperature record set on August 27, 1990. It also tied the record for highest dewpoint ever during the State Fair (77 degrees - August 28, 1955 and August 27, 1990). Minneapolis schools canceled all outdoor after-school athletics practices during this period.  The August 26th high of 96 degrees in the Twin Cities broke the 94-degree record set in 1948.||In Hennepin County, from the 25th through the 29th, there were 28 people who were treated for heat related illnesses, either as walk-ins at emergency rooms, or transported by ambulance to hospitals.   There were no known reports of heat illnesses treated outside Hennepin or Ramsey Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across the area rose above 90 degrees during the afternoon of Sunday, August 25th.  As dew point rose into the 70s, the heat indices surpassed 100 degrees for several hours during the afternoon, topping out around 105 degrees. During the evening, heat indices held above 80 degrees.  The heat and humidity continued through Thursday afternoon before temperatures fell into the 70s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Saturday, August, 31st, a complex of thunderstorms developed across southeast North Dakota, and moved into west central Minnesota during the late afternoon hours. These storms moved southeast across mainly western and southern Minnesota and produced large hail and damaging winds. Most of the severe storms were concentrated along the Minnesota River Valley during the early evening hours as a bow echo moved southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust occurred at the Redwood Falls Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in far northeast South Dakota, and moved east-southeast across west-central, and into central and east-central Minnesota during the afternoon and evening hours of Tuesday, August 6th.||One of the storms was long-lived and produced a swath of very large hail from Morris, southeast to the Twin Cities Metro area aninto Wisconsin. Numerous vehicles and homes were damaged from golf ball, up to baseball size hail in the southern metropolitan area.  In Bloomington, hail smashed windows in the Wells Fargo Plaza, leaving broken glass in the 24-story building's entry ways. The storms also brought high winds, which knocked over large trees in areas across the Metro area. Xcel Energy reported over 70,000 customers from St. Cloud through the Twin Cities area were without power during the height of the storm.

Thunderstorms developed during the evening of October 4th ahead of warm front that was lifting north out of Iowa. These storms produced locally heavy rains that caused flash flooding to occur across portions of Mower, Fillmore, Winona and Houston Counties. Mud slides closed roads between Pickwick and the city of Winona (Winona County) and washed out a portion of a county road northwest of Lanesboro (Fillmore County). Almost 6 inches of rain fell in about a six hour period in Preston (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In almost a six hour period, 5.85 inches of rain fell in Preston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms developed Thursday late afternoon and evening, June 20th, mostly in west central Minnesota, and moved eastward. These storms produced some penny to quarter size hail, but the main event was damaging straight line winds and torrential rainfall during the late evening and overnight period. These storms moved across central and east central Minnesota after midnight, with some redevelopment in far south central Minnesota toward daybreak. ||Flash flooding was observed in portions of west central and central Minnesota where locally three to five inches of rain fell in a short period of time. The towns of Morris, Glenwood, Starbuck, Sauk Centre and Long Prairie were the hardest hit with numerous flooded roadways. ||Wind damage came in two different waves, the first was associated with embedded bow-echoes that moved across central Minnesota, while the second wave came from outflow from previous thunderstorms.||Strong outflow winds from previous thunderstorms affected four counties in central Minnesota, producing a wide area of damage. Winds exceeded 40 mph for nearly two hours in portions of Todd and Stearns Counties.  Peak gusts include 63 mph at Sauk Centre (Stearns County) at 0200, 60 mph in Clearwater (Wright County) at 0240, 55 mph at Rice (Benton County) at 0200, and 54 mph at Long Prairie (Todd County) at 0235.   A business in Long Prairie lost a roof during the height of the high winds. Debris was blown to the south, landing on cars in a parking lot, and numerous trees were blown down around Long Prairie.  Trees and power lines were downed across portions of Todd, Benton, Stearns and Wright Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees, some over two feet in diameter, were blown down across the a large portion of St. Cloud, Minnesota. Some of the trees were entirely uprooted, and power was out in parts of St. Cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe storms near Redwood Falls and Albert Lea moved northeast across east central and southeast Minnesota during the early morning hours of Sunday, June 23rd.  After 2 am, a line of severe storms developed across southwest Minnesota and raced eastward rapidly and produced occasional large hail and damaging wind gusts across southern Minnesota. Due to several rounds of thunderstorms moving across the region, plus heavy rainfall during the previous two days, flash flooding developed in the morning around Glencoe and eastern Wright County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 1 foot diameter tree branch split from a tree near Fairmont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail at numerous locations in southwest Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of June 21st, with one brief tornado also reported. There was also flash flooding in Jackson County, which continued after midnight into the early morning hours of June 22nd.

Thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary from earlier thunderstorms across east central Minnesota during the morning of Wednesday, June 12th. Earlier in the day, thunderstorms across the western and southern parts of the Twin Cities received locally 2 to 3.5 inches of rainfall in a three hour period. ||By the afternoon, thunderstorms developed southwest of Mankato, and moved east-southeast across southern Blue Earth, Faribault, and Freeborn counties before moving into north central Iowa by the early evening. The most intense part of the thunderstorms occurred between Minnesota Lake, Wells and Alden, Minnesota, where severe winds, very large hail and flooding were reported. A barn, and several homes were damaged between Minnesota Lake and Wells where winds were measured above 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball size hail caused damage to area roofs in Wells, Minnesota.

Thunderstorms produced large hail at numerous locations in Cottonwood and Jackson Counties in southwest Minnesota on the late afternoon of May 17th. There were also two tornadoes reported, though no damage was reported with either.
Several reports of large hail were received in the Euclid, Minnesota area.
Melting snow caused overland flooding across portions of northwest Minnesota. Many rural roads were closed due to high water from the melting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed over the Devils Lake region along a cold frontal boundary and low pressure system. Storms also initiated along the warm front which extended down into southeast North Dakota. Afternoon temperatures topped out in the low to mid 80s with dew points around 70. By mid evening of the 25th the storm tracked into the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Many reports of large hail, strong winds, and heavy rain were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least one large oak tree and several large tree branches were blown down in and near Hallock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms over northeast Minnesota brought large hail and damaging winds on August 29th.  One thunderstorm produced an EF-1 tornado.  The tornado damage path began around 6:28 pm 1.7 miles north-northwest of Remer, MN and ended around 6:55 pm 5.0 miles west-southwest of Hill City, MN.  The tornado path was 12.8 miles long and occurred over about 27 minutes.  The damage path was through a rural, forested area of northeastern Cass County and northwestern Aitkin County, but it narrowly missed the town of Remer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado damage path began 1.7 miles north-northwest of Remer, MN.  The tornado took a path through a rural, forested area, but narrowly missed the town of Remer.  The damage was initially EF-0, but EF-1 damage was observed starting near Birch Lake to the county border between Cass and Aitkin Counties.  The damage was mainly to trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms rolled across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of May 19th. The first round of storms produced wind damage from the northeast corner of Mower County into Winona and Wabasha Counties during the afternoon. The second round of storms produced additional wind damage across primarily Winona County later that evening. In addition, the heavy rain from these repeated storms caused flash flooding to occur near Le Roy and Adams (Mower County) as water from streams flowed over several roads that prompted sandbagging.  Other flash flooding near Rose Creek (Mower County) caused significant flooding at a campground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Whitewater River out of its banks in the Whitewater State Park. The river crested less than a half foot over the flood stage at 13.9 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding to parts of northeast Minnesota on July 18th and early July 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust knocked down a large tree onto power lines on Highway 1 near mile marker 290.

In addition, several farms, fields and flood prone areas had standing water for several hours after 2 to 3 inches of rain fell in a short period of time.
